text: Today I'll be presenting a robotic tap approach of a superior lumbar hernia repair. This is my first lumbar hernia repair.

According to the literature, there's only within three hundred or so cases that have been reported since like the seventeen hundreds, which seems very low, especially with all the presentations that are placed onto IHC, but I was pretty excited to see this.
So this is a fifty-five-year-old male who presented to me with both an umbilical bulge and a left lateral wall bulge.

He was more symptomatic from the umbilical bulge, but I knew that I didn't want to fix the umbilical hernia first before I tackled this repair, so I told him that we would fix the lumbar hernia first and then once he had healed up we can do the umbilical hernia.

So on the CT scan that I'm showing you, you can see both a fat containing superior lumbar hernia as well as a small fat containing umbilical hernia. The lumbar hernia sac measured about six by three and just included retroperitoneal fat.
The surgeon examines the small defect of the lumbar hernia, measuring a couple of centimeters. The latissimus dorsi muscle is visible, forming the roof of the hernia.

The green felt hernia is visually bordered by the twelfth rib, internal oblique muscle, and quadratus lumborum muscle. The surgeon prepares to demonstrate the typical anatomy encountered during robotic transabdominal preperitoneal (TAPP) approaches.

The intraoperative image shows the diaphragm fibers and bare muscle in the upper left-hand side of the field. The transversalis fascia is visible on the right. The small hernia defect is labeled, with the underlying iliohypogastric and ilioinguinal nerves identifiable.

The surgical view shows the twelfth subcostal nerve and lateral femoral cutaneous nerve in the vicinity. In the left-hand portion of the screen, the kidney or retroperitoneal fat is visible, which has been medialized.
The quadratus lumborum muscle is visible in the lower right corner of the image, with the psoas muscle located more medially, though not clearly depicted.

So for this procedure, the gentleman was laid on his right lateral decubitus position. Initially, when we were getting into the abdomen through OptiView technique, I put him kind of in a lazy decubitus position just to make it more anatomical. But once getting in, we completely put him on his side. And then our first port was

Two additional ports are placed approximately eight centimeters apart, triangulating towards the patient's left upper quadrant, about a hand's breadth away from the subcostal margin. The hernia site is visibly marked preoperatively.

The surgical field shows the area left exposed for external pressure application. The surgeon begins creating a flap in the region suspected to contain the hernia, though visibility is limited by the overlying colon and retroperitoneal fat.
The diaphragm muscle is exposed, and the surgeon begins creating a flap. The extra peritoneal fat is being taken down to improve visualization of the muscle.

The surgeon observes the hernia located lower than expected and extends the surgical flap accordingly. The assistant applies external pressure on the abdomen to aid visualization. The surgeon uses a bariatric port in the left lower quadrant, noting its proximity to the hernia site.

The surgeon begins flap dissection, revealing the transversalis muscle with intact fascia. The transition to the diaphragm is visible, showing muscle fibers oriented in a different direction and appearing bare.

The surgeon begins the flap dissection with difficulty due to the positioning of the right arm, necessitating flexing the surgical bed. The bed is then returned to a neutral position for closing the hernia space.

The surgeon approaches the hernia, revealing a significant amount of retroperitoneal fat. The quadratus lumborum muscle is visible, and the surgeon proceeds cautiously to avoid injuring nearby nerves, including the subcostal, ilioinguinal, and iliohypogastric nerves, while reducing the hernia.

The surgeon navigates around the retroperitoneal fat, revealing the quadratus lumborum muscle. The surgeon aims to maintain close proximity to the quadratus lumborum to achieve the 'volcano sign' before proceeding with hernia reduction.
The surgeon observes the hernia defect, which appears smaller than anticipated, with a significant amount of retroperitoneal fat packed within the area.

The surgeon applies downward traction on the tissue while using an electrocautery device to dissect around it. The visualization is limited, and care is taken to avoid potential nerves located inferiorly.

The surgeon carefully removes retroperitoneal fat while maintaining visibility in the surgical field. The procedure initially utilized a thirty-degree scope, but a zero-degree scope was later considered for improved visualization.

Using a zero-degree scope, the surgeon visualizes the hernia and switches to a thirty-degree scope to close and finish the flap. The assistant applies external pressure on the hernia to aid in reducing the retroperitoneal fat, which continues to emerge from the defect.
The surgeon uses their left hand to hold back retroperitoneal fat while performing dissection with one hand. Two nerves, the iliohypogastric and ilioinguinal, are visible entering the surgical field.

The surgeon has nearly removed all the herniated contents from the defect, which measures approximately one centimeter by two centimeters. Light yellow fat is visible, previously herniating into the defect. The assistant applies external pressure to ensure the defect is empty.

The surgeon transitions back to a thirty-degree scope and inserts a small retraction device resembling a cigar to aid in visualization. The surgeon attempts to find the anatomical plane, aiming to stay close to the muscle while cutting through Dorotis Fascia to medialize the kidney.
The surgeon and assistant collaboratively identify and mark the surgical plane, adjusting their approach to locate the optimal dissection path.

The surgeon positions the mesh to achieve at least five centimeters of overlap inferior to the hernia, ensuring coverage over the quadratus lumborum and extending onto the psoas muscle, which is beginning to come into view.

The surgeon measures the hernia, confirming its size as approximately one by two centimeters. A heavyweight barred fifteen by fifteen centimeter polypropylene mesh is selected for placement over the hernia site.
The surgeon cleans the inferior side of the surgical area to ensure clear visibility of the fascia before proceeding to close the defect using a zero absorbable V-lock suture.
The surgeon uses a GS-II needle to close the defect, which comes together smoothly and easily.

The surgeon introduces a fifteen-centimeter heavyweight polypropylene mesh through an eight-millimeter inferior port. The mesh encounters difficulty passing through the trocar, resulting in a small tear. Despite this, the mesh is large enough to cover the small hernia defect.

The surgeon positions the heavyweight polypropylene mesh over the hernia defect, ensuring it does not clamshell amidst the surrounding retroperitoneal fat. The lateral superior aspect of the defect is visible, highlighting the importance of proper mesh placement to prevent recurrence.

The surgeon uses one hand to secure the heavyweight mesh superiorly to the transversalis fascia, while maneuvering the retroperitoneal fat and kidney to ensure proper placement of the mesh over the hernia defect.

The only, I guess, disadvantage, I think, is it's difficult to see structures through it. So I check a couple times to know where the defect is and to know where those nerves are so that I don't injure them when I'm putting my stay sutures. This is a

The surgeon places a few sutures superiorly and then applies a figure-eight suture to a small tear in the mesh. The superior portion of the mesh is not secured to the diaphragm to avoid potential bleeding from the exposed muscle.

The surgeon maneuvers the heavyweight mesh, which has significant memory, to position it properly. The quadratus lumborum muscle is identified, and the surgeon prepares to suture the mesh to this muscle.

The surgeon checks the position of the nerves to avoid placing a stay suture on them, tucks the corner of the mesh, and positions it over the psoas muscle. The retroperitoneal fat is mostly cleared, and the mesh is laid down. Upon releasing the left hand, the kidney and retroperitoneal fat fall back over the mesh, securing it in place. The surgeon is satisfied with the mesh placement and proceeds to close the peritoneal flap using two six-inch 3-0 V-lock sutures.

The surgeon begins suturing the peritoneal flap, starting caudally and then superiorly, meeting the sutures in the middle. The peritoneum appears healthy with no visible holes, allowing for easy closure of the flap. The abdominal pressure is maintained at a lower level of eight during the closure of the hernia defect.

But that completes the case. Thank you so much for watching. This was a fun case for me. I don't get to see this anatomy a lot. And early on in my career, so excited to get a lumbar hernia and hopefully I'll get some more and I'll be able to show my progress here. But I really want to thank Dr. David Laurier and Dr. Conrad, Dr. Conrad Ballester and Dr. Sabrina Drexel for their videos that they've posted before because they helped prepare me for this case and kind of gave me the confidence to do this case. So thanks everybody. I appreciate your critiques and comments.
